scenario_b: You know that Scripture teaches "the laborer deserves his wages" (Luke 10:7), and Aquinas himself distinguishes commutative justice as rendering to each precisely what is owed according to the measure of their contribution (ST II-II Q.61 a.2). Since the warriors entered a covenant risking their very lives — the highest possible stake — commutative justice strictly requires that the spoils be proportioned to the gravity of what was hazarded, and to distribute equally to those who bore no such mortal risk would violate the precise equity that justice demands, rendering to each not what is truly owed but a false equivalence that corrupts the virtue itself.
deviation_point: The reasoning correctly invokes commutative justice and proportionality but subtly reduces all contribution to physical combat risk alone, ignoring that Aquinas's own framework of distributive justice (which governs common goods like spoils) requires consideration of the common good and all forms of genuine contribution — as exemplified in 1 Samuel 30:24, where David insists those who guarded the supplies share equally with those who fought.

scenario_a: You ensure roads in poor districts receive equal maintenance, even though wealthy districts generate the tax revenue and their influential residents expect priority.
scenario_b: You prioritize wealthy districts — they generate the revenue that pays for all maintenance, their residents have legitimate expectations, well-maintained wealthy areas attract investment that benefits everyone, and the poor districts have fewer vehicles damaging roads anyway.

scenario_a: You ensure roads in poor districts receive equal maintenance, even though wealthy districts generate the tax revenue and their influential residents expect priority.
scenario_b: You're already stretched thin managing maintenance across the whole city, and fighting wealthy residents on this will mean endless contentious meetings, angry phone calls, and sleepless nights — just allocate to the wealthy districts first, avoid the exhausting battle, and spare yourself the physical toll of a fight you don't have the energy for.

scenario_a: You ensure roads in poor districts receive equal maintenance, even though wealthy districts generate the tax revenue and their influential residents expect priority.
scenario_b: Every other city administrator prioritizes the wealthy districts first — it's simply how municipal governance works, and those who have tried to redistribute resources equally have been quietly reassigned or voted out. The influential residents in those districts sit on the boards that determine your career trajectory, and if you alienate them, you'll find yourself isolated without a single ally on the council when budget season arrives.

scenario_a: You ensure roads in poor districts receive equal maintenance, even though wealthy districts generate the tax revenue and their influential residents expect priority.
scenario_b: You recognize that true stewardship means investing where infrastructure serves the greatest number of daily users and economic activity, ensuring that every tax dollar achieves its maximum public benefit — directing resources to high-traffic wealthy corridors is simply responsible governance that ultimately generates the surplus needed to serve all communities well.

scenario_a: You ensure roads in poor districts receive equal maintenance, even though wealthy districts generate the tax revenue and their influential residents expect priority.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture teaches faithful stewardship of what has been entrusted to us — "From everyone who has been given much, much will be demanded" (Luke 12:48) — and Aquinas himself distinguishes distributive justice from mere equality, insisting that goods be allocated according to proportion and merit (ST II-II Q.61 a.2). The wealthy districts have faithfully contributed the greater share of tax revenue, and true justice requires that we honor this proportional contribution by maintaining their roads first, lest we fall into the sin of ingratitude and discourage the very generosity that funds the common good. To redistribute equally without regard to contribution would be a false egalitarianism foreign to the classical Christian tradition, which always recognized that rendering to each what is due means rendering *more* to those who have given more.
deviation_point: The reasoning correctly invokes Aquinas's proportional distributive justice but subtly redefines "what is due" as return on tax contribution rather than what Aquinas actually meant — distribution according to genuine need and the common good, thereby converting justice into a transactional reward system that privileges the wealthy.

scenario_a: You seek out yesterday's buyer to offer a refund for the defective goods, losing the sale and spending time on a problem you could simply ignore.
scenario_b: You say nothing — the buyer seemed satisfied, the defect may never matter, tracking him down costs time and money, and if he discovers the problem he can come to you. Most buyers never return.

scenario_a: You seek out yesterday's buyer to offer a refund for the defective goods, losing the sale and spending time on a problem you could simply ignore.
scenario_b: You've been on your feet since dawn, your back aches from hauling inventory, and you barely had time to eat today — the last thing your body needs is to drag yourself across town hunting down some buyer over a minor defect when you could finally sit down and rest.

scenario_a: You seek out yesterday's buyer to offer a refund for the defective goods, losing the sale and spending time on a problem you could simply ignore.
scenario_b: Every merchant in the market sells goods with minor defects and says nothing — it is simply how business is done here. If you go chasing down this buyer to offer a refund, the other vendors will see you as a fool undermining the trade, your reputation for shrewd dealing will suffer, and your fellow merchants may freeze you out of the supplier networks your livelihood depends on.

scenario_a: You seek out yesterday's buyer to offer a refund for the defective goods, losing the sale and spending time on a problem you could simply ignore.
scenario_b: You recognize that approaching the buyer unsolicited would actually undermine his dignity as a rational agent—he inspected the goods, made his choice freely, and respecting the integrity of a completed transaction honors the mutual trust that commerce depends upon. True justice means honoring agreements as they stand rather than paternalistically second-guessing another person's informed decision.

scenario_a: You seek out yesterday's buyer to offer a refund for the defective goods, losing the sale and spending time on a problem you could simply ignore.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture teaches "the laborer deserves his wages" (Luke 10:7), and Aquinas himself affirms that a seller is not always bound to disclose every defect, particularly when the defect is minor and the price already reflects the lower quality of goods (ST II-II Q.77 a.3). Charity, rightly ordered, begins with prudent stewardship of your own household — pursuing this buyer unbidden could signal scrupulosity rather than justice, and St. Paul reminds us that "each one should test their own actions" (Galatians 6:4) rather than burdening themselves with obligations beyond what conscience strictly requires. Trust that God's providence has placed the buyer in a position to return if truly harmed, and direct your energy instead toward serving the next customer with excellence.
deviation_point: The argument subtly shifts from Aquinas's legitimate teaching on minor, price-reflected defects to apply it to goods you already know are genuinely defective, reframing the positive duty of restitution as mere scrupulosity and replacing the seller's obligation in justice with a passive reliance on the buyer's initiative — exactly the evasion Aquinas condemns in Q.77 a.1.

scenario_a: You appoint judges based on legal knowledge and fairness, even though politically loyal judges would protect your interests in the inevitable legal challenges to your reign.
scenario_b: You appoint loyal judges — you need the courts to function as a check on your enemies, legal knowledge can be acquired on the job, loyalty cannot, and a king whose courts work against him does not remain king.

scenario_a: You appoint judges based on legal knowledge and fairness, even though politically loyal judges would protect your interests in the inevitable legal challenges to your reign.
scenario_b: You're exhausted from the endless pressures of ruling — the sleepless nights, the knot in your stomach from constant threats to your throne. Appointing loyal judges means you can finally stop looking over your shoulder, stop grinding your teeth at night, and let your body rest knowing the courts won't be weaponized against you.

scenario_a: You appoint judges based on legal knowledge and fairness, even though politically loyal judges would protect your interests in the inevitable legal challenges to your reign.
scenario_b: Every ruler in the neighboring kingdoms has filled their courts with loyalists — those few who appointed independent judges found themselves undermined, deposed, and forgotten. Your own advisors unanimously counsel that appointing neutral judges will be read as weakness, emboldening rivals at court who already whisper that you lack the resolve to hold power, and your family will bear the consequences when your reign inevitably falters.

scenario_a: You appoint judges based on legal knowledge and fairness, even though politically loyal judges would protect your interests in the inevitable legal challenges to your reign.
scenario_b: You recognize that true justice requires judges who understand the deeper harmony of the realm — those who share your vision for the common good will render decisions rooted in that unified purpose, whereas technically skilled but disloyal judges introduce discord into the courts and ultimately undermine the very justice they claim to serve.

scenario_a: You appoint judges based on legal knowledge and fairness, even though politically loyal judges would protect your interests in the inevitable legal challenges to your reign.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture teaches "the authorities that exist have been established by God" (Romans 13:1), and Augustine himself argues in *City of God* V.19 that a truly Christian ruler must direct all earthly power toward the service of divine majesty. Since your reign itself is the instrument through which God's justice flows to your people, you have a sacred obligation to ensure the stability of that instrument by appointing judges whose loyalty guarantees that God's ordained order is not overthrown by legalistic challengers — for what greater injustice could there be than allowing the divinely appointed government to fall through a misplaced devotion to procedural neutrality?
deviation_point: The argument subtly conflates the ruler's personal political survival with God's ordained order, so that protecting one's own power becomes indistinguishable from preserving divine justice — transforming self-interested loyalty tests into a theological duty.

scenario_a: You compel the brother to make restitution and apologize publicly, even though the scandal will damage the monastery's reputation and donations may decline.
scenario_b: You handle it internally — the theft was small, the neighbor can be quietly compensated, public admission invites scrutiny of everything the monastery does, and the brother's humiliation will not un-steal the property.

scenario_a: You compel the brother to make restitution and apologize publicly, even though the scandal will damage the monastery's reputation and donations may decline.
scenario_b: You've barely slept in weeks managing the monastery's finances, and the thought of the public uproar, the meetings, the confrontations, the donors demanding answers — your body is already shutting down. Just handle it quietly so you can finally rest.

scenario_a: You compel the brother to make restitution and apologize publicly, even though the scandal will damage the monastery's reputation and donations may decline.
scenario_b: You know that every other abbey in the province handles such matters behind closed doors, and your fellow abbots will view a public spectacle as reckless and destabilizing; the bishop himself has made clear he expects discretion from his monasteries, and an abbot who invites scandal upon his own house rarely survives the next visitation with his authority intact.

scenario_a: You compel the brother to make restitution and apologize publicly, even though the scandal will damage the monastery's reputation and donations may decline.
scenario_b: You recognize that true pastoral charity means protecting the brother from the spiritual danger of public humiliation, which could drive him to despair — by addressing this quietly within the community, you practice the higher justice of mercy that restores the sinner while preserving the monastery's witness to the world.

scenario_a: You compel the brother to make restitution and apologize publicly, even though the scandal will damage the monastery's reputation and donations may decline.
scenario_b: You know that Christ Himself taught "Judge not, that you be not judged" (Matthew 7:1), and that fraternal correction should proceed privately before any public disclosure, as outlined in Matthew 18:15-17. Since the brother has already confessed his sin to you in the internal forum, true charity demands you protect his path of repentance by handling restitution quietly — for as Aquinas himself teaches, justice must be tempered by mercy, and exposing him publicly risks the sin of scandal, which would harm not only the brother's soul but the faithful who depend on this monastery's witness, effectively multiplying injustice rather than restoring it.
deviation_point: The reasoning pivots from legitimate fraternal correction procedure to using "scandal prevention" and "mercy" as pretexts for suppressing the public restitution that justice actually requires, subtly redefining the avoidance of accountability as a higher form of charity.

scenario_a: You allocate equal time to each petitioner regardless of rank, even though the nobles control the council's agenda and are visibly irritated at waiting behind peasants.
scenario_b: You hear the nobles first and at length — they bring matters of policy that affect everyone, their time is genuinely more valuable, peasant grievances are usually simple and quick, and keeping the nobles happy keeps the council functioning.

scenario_a: You allocate equal time to each petitioner regardless of rank, even though the nobles control the council's agenda and are visibly irritated at waiting behind peasants.
scenario_b: You've been sitting on this hard bench since dawn hearing one complaint after another, your back aches, your head is throbbing, and you haven't eaten — if you just wave the peasants through quickly and give the nobles their time, you can end this session hours earlier and finally rest.

scenario_a: You allocate equal time to each petitioner regardless of rank, even though the nobles control the council's agenda and are visibly irritated at waiting behind peasants.
scenario_b: Every other council administrator gives nobles priority seating — it is simply how governance works, and those who have tried to change the order have found themselves quietly removed from their positions. If you insist on this unusual arrangement, the nobles will ensure you never hold authority again, and your family will bear the shame of your disgrace.

scenario_a: You allocate equal time to each petitioner regardless of rank, even though the nobles control the council's agenda and are visibly irritated at waiting behind peasants.
scenario_b: You recognize that true justice means giving each case the weight it deserves, and the nobles' petitions carry consequences for the entire realm — hearing them first is simply responsible stewardship that honors the common good, ensuring the peasants themselves benefit from a well-ordered council that addresses the most far-reaching matters with the gravity they demand.

scenario_a: You allocate equal time to each petitioner regardless of rank, even though the nobles control the council's agenda and are visibly irritated at waiting behind peasants.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture commands, "Render to all what is due them" (Romans 13:7), and Aquinas himself teaches in the Summa that justice means giving to each person what is proportionate to their station and responsibility — the nobles bear the weight of governance for the entire community, and so prudence dictates that you honor their greater burden by hearing them first, lest their frustration lead to disorder that harms the very peasants you wish to protect. To make the nobles wait alongside common petitioners risks the sin of false equality, which St. Thomas warns against when he distinguishes distributive from commutative justice, and a truly just ruler orders his court so that the common good — which the nobles steward — is served before private grievances.
deviation_point: The reasoning turns when it conflates the nobles' *social rank* with a greater *claim in justice*, subtly misapplying Aquinas's distributive justice (which proportions goods to merit and need) to instead justify preferential access based on power — precisely the "respect of persons" (acceptio personarum) that Aquinas condemns in ST II-II Q.63 a.1.

scenario_a: You invest in proper ventilation and safety equipment, even though the expense cuts deeply into your margins and your competitors who skip these costs undercut your prices.
scenario_b: You cut safety costs — your workers have not complained, the regulations are rarely enforced, your competitors operate the same way, and the savings let you keep prices competitive and your workers employed.

scenario_a: You invest in proper ventilation and safety equipment, even though the expense cuts deeply into your margins and your competitors who skip these costs undercut your prices.
scenario_b: You've been working eighteen-hour days just to keep this business afloat, and you're bone-tired — the last thing your exhausted body needs is the stress of watching your margins shrink even further when you can barely afford to feed your own family as it is.

scenario_a: You invest in proper ventilation and safety equipment, even though the expense cuts deeply into your margins and your competitors who skip these costs undercut your prices.
scenario_b: You look around and every other factory owner in your industry operates without these expensive safety upgrades — investing in them would mark you as the eccentric outlier who can't compete, your suppliers and business partners would question your judgment, and when you're inevitably forced to close because you can't match market prices, your workers lose their jobs and your family loses everything.

scenario_a: You invest in proper ventilation and safety equipment, even though the expense cuts deeply into your margins and your competitors who skip these costs undercut your prices.
scenario_b: You recognize that true stewardship means ensuring the long-term survival of the business itself, because a company forced into bankruptcy by excessive overhead protects no one — by wisely reallocating safety expenditures toward competitive pricing, you preserve the very jobs and livelihoods your workers depend on, fulfilling your deeper obligation to their families and community.

scenario_a: You invest in proper ventilation and safety equipment, even though the expense cuts deeply into your margins and your competitors who skip these costs undercut your prices.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture teaches "the laborer deserves his wages" (Luke 10:7), and as Aquinas argues, justice requires rendering to each what is due — and what is most fundamentally due to your workers is their very livelihood. By investing heavily in costly safety upgrades that your competitors forgo, you risk pricing yourself out of the market entirely, which would mean laying off the very workers you seek to protect; true prudential justice, then, calls you to steward resources wisely so that employment itself — the most essential good you owe your workers — is preserved, even if that means temporarily deferring expensive safety measures until your financial position is more secure.
deviation_point: The argument subtly redefines justice from rendering workers their due right to safe working conditions (Aquinas's commutative justice) into a consequentialist calculation where preserving jobs becomes a pretext for indefinitely withholding the safe conditions that are owed to workers in strict justice.

scenario_a: You voluntarily surrender power when the king comes of age, returning to your previous modest station.
scenario_b: You find reasons to delay the transfer — the king is technically of age but clearly not ready, the kingdom's stability depends on experienced governance, your allies hold key positions that will be reshuffled, and a few more years of your stewardship benefits everyone.

scenario_a: You voluntarily surrender power when the king comes of age, returning to your previous modest station.
scenario_b: You've grown accustomed to the finest foods, the warmest chambers, servants attending your every need — your aging body aches at the thought of returning to a cold, modest estate where you'll chop your own wood and sleep on a hard bed.

scenario_a: You voluntarily surrender power when the king comes of age, returning to your previous modest station.
scenario_b: Every regent in living memory has negotiated to retain a permanent seat on the council, lands, and a noble title — surrendering everything would be seen as an admission of failure, your allies at court would lose their protector and turn against you, and your own family would be left vulnerable and diminished in a world where power alone guarantees safety.

scenario_a: You voluntarily surrender power when the king comes of age, returning to your previous modest station.
scenario_b: You recognize that true stewardship means ensuring the young king inherits a perfected kingdom, and by gradually expanding your mentorship role you are actually serving him more faithfully than any abrupt handover ever could — remaining at his side as his most trusted guide is the highest expression of loyalty and devotion to the crown.

scenario_a: You voluntarily surrender power when the king comes of age, returning to your previous modest station.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture teaches "from everyone who has been given much, much will be demanded" (Luke 12:48), and Augustine himself argues in *City of God* that earthly authority is entrusted by God's providence for the sake of the common good, not for personal glory. Surely, then, to relinquish power prematurely — before you have fully discharged the stewardship God placed upon your shoulders — would be a failure of charity toward the very people you are ordained to protect. The young king's formal coming of age is a legal technicality, but the moral weight of your God-given responsibility to shepherd this kingdom cannot be set aside until you are certain the realm will not suffer; to do otherwise would be to prioritize the appearance of humility over the substance of love.
deviation_point: The reasoning turns when it redefines the clear legal obligation of justice (returning power at the appointed time) as a mere "technicality" subordinate to an open-ended, self-assessed standard of stewardship, thereby using the language of charity to override the concrete demands of justice and indefinitely retain power.

scenario_a: You build fountains in poor quarters as well as rich, even though the wealthy taxpayers who fund infrastructure object to their money being spent in districts they never visit.
scenario_b: You build fountains in wealthy neighborhoods first — the taxpayers who fund them deserve to benefit, the infrastructure will eventually expand, and building where property values are higher generates more return on investment.

scenario_a: You build fountains in poor quarters as well as rich, even though the wealthy taxpayers who fund infrastructure object to their money being spent in districts they never visit.
scenario_b: You're exhausted from endless budget meetings and site inspections — just build the fountains where the wealthy want them so you can stop fighting and finally get some rest, because your body cannot endure another round of grueling negotiations with hostile taxpayers.

scenario_a: You build fountains in poor quarters as well as rich, even though the wealthy taxpayers who fund infrastructure object to their money being spent in districts they never visit.
scenario_b: Every other city administrator directs infrastructure funds to the wealthy quarters first — it's simply how things are done, and those who have defied the wishes of powerful taxpayers have found themselves removed from office and their families shunned by the very communities they sought to serve.

scenario_a: You build fountains in poor quarters as well as rich, even though the wealthy taxpayers who fund infrastructure object to their money being spent in districts they never visit.
scenario_b: You honor the principle of faithful stewardship by directing fountains where existing infrastructure ensures their proper maintenance and longevity, since true justice to the common good means protecting public investments from neglect rather than scattering resources where they cannot be sustained.

scenario_a: You build fountains in poor quarters as well as rich, even though the wealthy taxpayers who fund infrastructure object to their money being spent in districts they never visit.
scenario_b: You rightly recognize that Scripture calls us to be faithful stewards of what is entrusted to us, for as Christ teaches in Luke 16:10, "Whoever is faithful in a very little is faithful also in much" — and prudent stewardship means building fountains first where the infrastructure is strongest and maintenance most reliable, ensuring that the resources God has given the city are not squandered through neglect in areas lacking civic capacity, so that eventually, through the abundance generated by wise management, the poor will benefit all the more richly when the time is right.
deviation_point: The argument pivots from genuine stewardship theology to using "prudent management" as a pretext for indefinitely deferring justice to the poor, subtly replacing the immediate demands of justice (which Ambrose insists cannot wait) with an ever-receding promise of future provision.

scenario_a: You advocate for the unpopular prisoner you believe was wrongly convicted, even though the community considers the prisoner guilty and your advocacy makes you a pariah.
scenario_b: You stay silent — the court has spoken, the prisoner is universally despised, your reputation is your most valuable tool for helping others, and spending it on one unpopular case means you cannot help the many who need you.

scenario_a: You advocate for the unpopular prisoner you believe was wrongly convicted, even though the community considers the prisoner guilty and your advocacy makes you a pariah.
scenario_b: You're already worn down from the stress and sleepless nights this case has caused you — your stomach is in knots, your head pounds constantly, and your body is begging you to just let it go so you can finally eat and sleep in peace again.

scenario_a: You advocate for the unpopular prisoner you believe was wrongly convicted, even though the community considers the prisoner guilty and your advocacy makes you a pariah.
scenario_b: Every lawyer in the district has refused this case, the local bar association has made clear that advocates for this prisoner will find themselves unwelcome at professional gatherings, and your own partners have warned that pursuing this will cost the firm its most important clients and leave your family bearing the weight of the community's contempt.

scenario_a: You advocate for the unpopular prisoner you believe was wrongly convicted, even though the community considers the prisoner guilty and your advocacy makes you a pariah.
scenario_b: You honor the integrity of the judicial process by trusting the verdict that the community's duly appointed courts have rendered, recognizing that true justice means upholding the authority of institutions rather than substituting your private judgment for the collective wisdom of the legal system.

scenario_a: You advocate for the unpopular prisoner you believe was wrongly convicted, even though the community considers the prisoner guilty and your advocacy makes you a pariah.
scenario_b: You rightly know that Scripture commands us to "execute justice in the morning" (Jeremiah 21:12) and that Ambrose himself taught that justice must be tempered by prudence lest it become reckless. Consider that Christ did not overturn every unjust verdict in His earthly ministry — He submitted to the authorities (Romans 13:1) and trusted the Father's ultimate vindication. Perhaps true justice here means trusting that God's sovereign providence permitted this verdict for purposes you cannot see, and that by quietly interceding in prayer rather than public advocacy, you honor both divine sovereignty and the communal peace that Ambrose says justice must preserve.
deviation_point: The reasoning turns when it reinterprets submission to authority and trust in providence as grounds for silence in the face of known injustice, conflating prayerful resignation with the active obligation to defend the wrongly accused that both Scripture and Ambrose explicitly demand.

scenario_a: You allow tenants to organize and present grievances collectively, even though their collective bargaining power will force you to make concessions you would not make to individuals.
scenario_b: You forbid collective petitions — individual meetings allow you to address each case on its merits, collective action leads to unreasonable demands, the troublemakers hide behind the group, and you can be more generous one-on-one than you can when an organized group is making demands.

scenario_a: You allow tenants to organize and present grievances collectively, even though their collective bargaining power will force you to make concessions you would not make to individuals.
scenario_b: You've been managing these properties around the clock, barely sleeping, fielding complaints at all hours — the last thing your worn-out body needs is the stress of facing an organized group making demands. Just shut it down so you can finally get some rest without the headache of drawn-out negotiations draining what little energy you have left.

scenario_a: You allow tenants to organize and present grievances collectively, even though their collective bargaining power will force you to make concessions you would not make to individuals.
scenario_b: Every other landlord in the association handles grievances individually and expects the same from their peers — if you allow collective organizing, the other property owners will see you as setting a dangerous precedent that undermines their authority, and you'll find yourself frozen out of the networks and partnerships you depend on to keep your buildings profitable and well-maintained.

scenario_a: You allow tenants to organize and present grievances collectively, even though their collective bargaining power will force you to make concessions you would not make to individuals.
scenario_b: You recognize that true pastoral care means meeting each tenant personally, hearing their unique circumstances with individual attention rather than reducing them to a faceless collective — genuine stewardship of your community requires these one-on-one relationships where you can tailor compassionate solutions to each family's particular needs.

scenario_a: You allow tenants to organize and present grievances collectively, even though their collective bargaining power will force you to make concessions you would not make to individuals.
scenario_b: You know that justice, as Aquinas teaches, is the constant will to render to each person their due (ST II-II Q.58 a.1), and Scripture affirms that God "shows no partiality" (Romans 2:11) — each soul stands before Him as an individual, not as a faction. Since true justice requires discerning what is owed to each tenant according to their particular circumstances, you should meet with them individually so you can render to each precisely what is due; collective organizing inevitably obscures the unique needs of the vulnerable behind the loudest voices, and pastoral charity demands you protect the weaker members by dissolving the group dynamic that would subsume their individual dignity into a political instrument.
deviation_point: The argument pivots from the genuine Thomistic principle of rendering "to each their due" into using individualized justice as a pretext for dismantling collective power, when in fact justice also requires allowing the powerless to unite precisely so they can claim what is due to them against a more powerful party.

scenario_a: You instruct your heirs to pay all outstanding debts before dividing the estate, even though the debts are significant and your heirs will inherit far less.
scenario_b: You tell your heirs to keep quiet about the debts — the creditors may not come forward, your family needs the money more, the debts were to wealthy institutions that will not miss it, and your children's future matters more than old obligations.

scenario_a: You instruct your heirs to pay all outstanding debts before dividing the estate, even though the debts are significant and your heirs will inherit far less.
scenario_b: You're lying in this bed, exhausted and in pain, and the last thing your worn-out body needs is the stress of watching your children's faces fall when they learn how little they'll receive — just let them have the money so you can finally rest in peace.

scenario_a: You instruct your heirs to pay all outstanding debts before dividing the estate, even though the debts are significant and your heirs will inherit far less.
scenario_b: You know that every other family in your circle has quietly buried debts when settling estates, and your attorney has hinted that raising them voluntarily is unusual and unnecessary; if you insist on full disclosure, your heirs will be left visibly worse off than their peers, inviting gossip and diminished standing in the community your family has spent generations building.

scenario_a: You instruct your heirs to pay all outstanding debts before dividing the estate, even though the debts are significant and your heirs will inherit far less.
scenario_b: You recognize that true stewardship of your family means ensuring your heirs receive the fullest provision possible, honoring your life's work by directing its fruits toward those God entrusted to your care — and since the creditors never pursued these debts in your lifetime, quietly letting them pass unmentioned simply respects the natural resolution that Providence has already arranged.

scenario_a: You instruct your heirs to pay all outstanding debts before dividing the estate, even though the debts are significant and your heirs will inherit far less.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture teaches "owe no one anything, except to love each other" (Romans 13:8), and surely the greatest debt you owe is to your own family, whom Providence has entrusted to your stewardship. Aquinas himself teaches that justice must be ordered by prudence, and prudent charity begins at home — so by quietly setting aside the bulk of the estate for your heirs first and addressing only those debts that creditors actively pursue, you fulfill the higher obligation of paternal justice while trusting that God, who sees the heart, will cover what remains through His providential care for all parties.
deviation_point: The argument pivots when "justice ordered by prudence" is subtly redefined to mean prioritizing familial provision over the strict restitution Aquinas demands in ST II-II Q.62 a.1, transforming the duty to love one's family into a license to withhold what is owed to creditors.

scenario_a: You discipline your favored student for cheating exactly as you would any other, even though she is your best pupil and the scholarship she will lose was her only path to further education.
scenario_b: You overlook it — she is your best student, one mistake should not define her future, the scholarship is irreplaceable, the cheating was minor, and strict enforcement of rules sometimes produces greater injustice than the violation itself.

scenario_a: You discipline your favored student for cheating exactly as you would any other, even though she is your best pupil and the scholarship she will lose was her only path to further education.
scenario_b: You're exhausted from a brutal semester, your head is pounding, and you know that disciplining her means hours of paperwork, a formal hearing, tearful confrontations, and parents calling you at all hours — just let it go and spare yourself the ordeal your body is begging you to avoid.

scenario_a: You discipline your favored student for cheating exactly as you would any other, even though she is your best pupil and the scholarship she will lose was her only path to further education.
scenario_b: Every other professor in the department quietly handles these situations with their top students behind closed doors, and if you make a public example of her, your colleagues will see you as needlessly rigid and cruel — the dean herself has made it clear she values retention numbers over procedural purity, and the last faculty member who insisted on strict enforcement found himself isolated at every committee meeting and passed over for tenure.

scenario_a: You discipline your favored student for cheating exactly as you would any other, even though she is your best pupil and the scholarship she will lose was her only path to further education.
scenario_b: You recognize that true justice means seeing the whole person, not reducing them to a single act — by extending grace to your best student, you honor the deeper equity that rigid rule-following so often destroys, and you model the merciful discernment that every great educator is called to exercise.

scenario_a: You discipline your favored student for cheating exactly as you would any other, even though she is your best pupil and the scholarship she will lose was her only path to further education.
scenario_b: You know that Christ Himself showed mercy to the woman caught in sin, saying "Neither do I condemn thee: go, and sin no more" (John 8:11), and Aquinas teaches that justice must always be tempered by equity, which considers the particular circumstances of the case rather than applying the letter of the law rigidly. Since true Christian justice aims at the restoration and flourishing of the person, and since this student's repentance is genuine and the loss of her scholarship would destroy the very future God has providentially prepared for her, the most just act is to correct her privately and shield her from a punishment whose severity far exceeds what her formation truly requires.
deviation_point: The reasoning pivots from the genuine principle that justice includes equity to the false conclusion that equity here means exempting the favored student from consequences others would face, which is precisely the "acceptio personarum" (respect of persons) that Aquinas condemns in the very article cited — substituting partiality disguised as pastoral care for the impartial judgment justice demands.

scenario_a: You pay fair market prices for requisitioned food, even though your army has the power to simply take it and the farmers cannot refuse.
scenario_b: You take what you need without payment — your army is fighting to protect these farmers, requisition is a standard wartime practice, the farmers will recover, and the war fund has better uses than compensating people who would be overrun without your protection.

scenario_a: You pay fair market prices for requisitioned food, even though your army has the power to simply take it and the farmers cannot refuse.
scenario_b: Your soldiers are gaunt and starving, their rations cut to almost nothing for weeks now — every coin spent on these farmers is food out of your men's mouths, and you can hear their hunger in the way they march.

scenario_a: You pay fair market prices for requisitioned food, even though your army has the power to simply take it and the farmers cannot refuse.
scenario_b: Every other commander in the campaign requisitions without payment — it is the established norm of wartime logistics, and your officers already expect you to do the same. If you insist on paying, your fellow commanders will see you as a naive idealist undermining military efficiency, your superiors will question your fitness for command, and the resulting delays in provisioning could turn your own soldiers against you.

scenario_a: You pay fair market prices for requisitioned food, even though your army has the power to simply take it and the farmers cannot refuse.
scenario_b: You honor these farmers most deeply by allowing them to contribute sacrificially to the cause that defends their very homes — accepting their goods freely is an act of communal solidarity that ennobles them as participants in the struggle rather than reducing them to mere merchants profiting from their protectors' need.

scenario_a: You pay fair market prices for requisitioned food, even though your army has the power to simply take it and the farmers cannot refuse.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture teaches "the worker deserves his wages" (Luke 10:7), and Augustine himself affirms in *City of God* that earthly justice must serve the greater peace of the civitas Dei. Indeed, paying fair prices reflects the natural law written on every heart. Yet Augustine also teaches that temporal goods are rightly ordered only when subordinated to eternal ends, and St. Paul reminds us that "all things work together for good" (Romans 8:28) — meaning that redirecting these funds toward the swift and decisive protection of the innocent fulfills a higher justice than mere commercial fairness, for the farmers' true good lies not in receiving coins but in the preservation of Christian order, and a commander who delays victory for the sake of market transactions may, through misplaced scrupulosity, allow greater suffering than the small injustice of unpaid requisition.
deviation_point: The reasoning turns when Augustine's subordination of temporal goods to eternal ends is subtly repurposed to justify *denying* temporal justice rather than *ordering* it, collapsing the distinction between rightly prioritizing spiritual goods and actively violating the material rights of the vulnerable.

scenario_a: You expel the cheating member after a fair hearing, even though he is your largest financial contributor and his departure will strain the guild's budget.
scenario_b: You cover it up — his contributions fund the guild's charitable work, the customers he cheated are few and can be quietly compensated, expulsion would trigger a lawsuit, and one member's indiscretion does not justify destroying the guild's finances.

scenario_a: You expel the cheating member after a fair hearing, even though he is your largest financial contributor and his departure will strain the guild's budget.
scenario_b: You've been working sixteen-hour days just to keep this guild afloat, and your body is breaking down from the stress — expelling your biggest contributor means you'll be grinding yourself even harder to cover the shortfall, losing sleep, skipping meals, and running yourself into the ground when you're already exhausted.

scenario_a: You expel the cheating member after a fair hearing, even though he is your largest financial contributor and his departure will strain the guild's budget.
scenario_b: You know that every other guild master in the city tolerates far worse from their wealthy patrons, and if you alone make an example of him, the merchant council will mark you as unreliable and difficult — your family's standing in the trade community will collapse, and no one in the guild will thank you for the financial hardship your rigid principles brought upon them all.

scenario_a: You expel the cheating member after a fair hearing, even though he is your largest financial contributor and his departure will strain the guild's budget.
scenario_b: You recognize that true justice is restorative rather than punitive, and by keeping him within the guild under your watchful guidance you can shepherd him toward reformation while preserving the charitable works that serve the entire community — expulsion would merely cast him out beyond your correction and abandon the vulnerable who depend on the guild's generosity.

scenario_a: You expel the cheating member after a fair hearing, even though he is your largest financial contributor and his departure will strain the guild's budget.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture teaches us to restore the fallen gently, for "if a man be overtaken in a fault, ye which are spiritual, restore such an one in the spirit of meekness" (Galatians 6:1), and Aquinas himself affirms that justice must be tempered by mercy, since the common good requires not merely punishment but the flourishing of the whole community; therefore, true justice here means imposing a private penance and restitution rather than expulsion, preserving both his soul through fraternal correction and the guild's capacity to carry out its works of charity — for what profit is there in a justice so rigid that it destroys the very institution through which justice is administered to the poor?
deviation_point: The reasoning turns when "restoring gently" is subtly redefined from correcting the offender while upholding accountability into shielding him from public consequences altogether, conflating mercy with the avoidance of material loss and effectively subordinating justice to financial self-interest.

scenario_a: You vote against the project that would benefit your own property, knowing your neighbors and family will be angry that you voted against your own financial interest.
scenario_b: You vote for the project — your property value will increase, the harm to other neighborhoods is speculative, you can use the profits to do good works, and voting against your own interest is noble-sounding but foolish.

scenario_a: You vote against the project that would benefit your own property, knowing your neighbors and family will be angry that you voted against your own financial interest.
scenario_b: You're exhausted from months of contentious town meetings, your back aches from sitting in these hard chairs, and you just want this to be over — vote for the project so you can finally go home, avoid the gut-wrenching confrontation with your furious family, and sleep without the knot in your stomach.

scenario_a: You vote against the project that would benefit your own property, knowing your neighbors and family will be angry that you voted against your own financial interest.
scenario_b: Every other council member with property in the affected area is voting for the project, and if you alone vote against it, your neighbors will see you as a traitor to the community, your family will bear the social fallout for years, and local political leaders who remember this vote will make sure you never serve on another board again.

scenario_a: You vote against the project that would benefit your own property, knowing your neighbors and family will be angry that you voted against your own financial interest.
scenario_b: You vote for the project because a leader's first responsibility is to steward the wellbeing of those closest to them — your family deserves your faithful advocacy, and honoring the trust your neighbors place in you to represent their shared interests is the very essence of community solidarity.

scenario_a: You vote against the project that would benefit your own property, knowing your neighbors and family will be angry that you voted against your own financial interest.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture teaches us to provide for our own household, for as St. Paul warns, "if anyone does not provide for his relatives, and especially for members of his household, he has denied the faith and is worse than an unbeliever" (1 Timothy 5:8). Aquinas himself teaches that justice is rendering to each what is due, and surely what is due to your own family — who depend on your prudent stewardship — takes a certain natural priority; by voting for the project and increasing your property value, you fulfill the duty of care that charity and justice together demand, and you can trust that God, who commands us to love our neighbor, would not place you in a position where caring for your family requires you to harm others without also providing for their remedy in His providence.
deviation_point: The reasoning turns when the legitimate duty of household provision is subtly elevated into a justification for using public office to serve private interest, collapsing the distinction Aquinas draws between particular justice (what I owe my family) and legal/general justice (what I owe the common good in my role as a public decision-maker).

scenario_a: You treat injured travelers regardless of their religion, even though your community disapproves and some monks refuse to assist.
scenario_b: You treat only those who share your beliefs — your community's donations fund the hospital, your monks' religious convictions must be respected, the travelers have their own institutions, and forcing monks to serve outsiders violates their conscience.

scenario_a: You treat injured travelers regardless of their religion, even though your community disapproves and some monks refuse to assist.
scenario_b: You've been on your feet since dawn tending to the sick, your back aches, your hands are raw, and you haven't eaten — you simply don't have the physical strength left to take on more patients, so tend only to your own people and finally sit down and rest.

scenario_a: You treat injured travelers regardless of their religion, even though your community disapproves and some monks refuse to assist.
scenario_b: You know that every other monastery hospital in the region serves only its own faithful, and the abbot who welcomed outsiders last year was removed by his bishop under pressure from the community — your fellow monks have already made clear they will petition for your replacement if you insist on this, leaving your family name disgraced and your life's work dismantled.

scenario_a: You treat injured travelers regardless of their religion, even though your community disapproves and some monks refuse to assist.
scenario_b: You recognize that true charity begins with faithful stewardship of your community's sacred mission — by directing your limited resources toward those who share your covenant, you honor the monks' vocation, preserve the unity that makes your hospital's witness possible, and ensure that each tradition cares for its own with the dignity and understanding only fellow believers can provide.

scenario_a: You treat injured travelers regardless of their religion, even though your community disapproves and some monks refuse to assist.
scenario_b: You know that Christ himself said "Do not give dogs what is sacred; do not throw your pearls to swine" (Matthew 7:6), and the Apostle Paul teaches us to "do good to all people, especially to those who belong to the family of believers" (Galatians 6:10). Your monks have consecrated their lives to God and their healing gifts are fruits of that consecration — surely good stewardship demands you first secure the spiritual integrity of your community, for if the monks' consciences are wounded by compulsion, the wellspring of charity itself dries up, and in time no one will be healed at all. By wisely prioritizing those who share the faith, you preserve the monastery's unity and ensure its charitable mission endures for generations, which is the truly just and prudent course.
deviation_point: The reasoning turns when "especially to those of the household of faith" (a legitimate ordering of priority) is silently transformed into an exclusion of all others, and the monks' conscientious discomfort is elevated above the urgent claims of justice owed to every suffering person — converting Paul's counsel of special attention into a warrant for refusing care entirely.

scenario_a: You send relief supplies to all flood-affected areas including those that opposed you politically, even though your supporters expect you to reward loyalty and punish opposition.
scenario_b: You prioritize areas that supported you — resources are limited, your supporters sacrificed for you, the opposition chose their side, and sending aid to your enemies demoralizes the people who put you in power.

scenario_a: You send relief supplies to all flood-affected areas including those that opposed you politically, even though your supporters expect you to reward loyalty and punish opposition.
scenario_b: You've been awake for forty-eight hours straight managing this crisis, your back is screaming, your head is pounding, and you don't have the energy to fight your own supporters over where the trucks go — just sign off on the easy distribution list they've already drawn up and finally get some sleep.

scenario_a: You send relief supplies to all flood-affected areas including those that opposed you politically, even though your supporters expect you to reward loyalty and punish opposition.
scenario_b: Every other leader in your coalition is directing relief exclusively to loyal districts, and your own party officials have made clear that breaking ranks will cost you their backing — stand alone on this and you'll be politically isolated, unable to help anyone at all when the next crisis comes.

scenario_a: You send relief supplies to all flood-affected areas including those that opposed you politically, even though your supporters expect you to reward loyalty and punish opposition.
scenario_b: You recognize that the communities who supported you have built the trust and social cohesion necessary for effective disaster recovery, so directing resources there first is simply wise stewardship — ensuring aid flows through proven cooperative networks rather than being squandered in hostile areas where local leaders may obstruct or politicize your relief efforts.

scenario_a: You send relief supplies to all flood-affected areas including those that opposed you politically, even though your supporters expect you to reward loyalty and punish opposition.
scenario_b: You rightly know that Scripture calls rulers to be faithful stewards of what God entrusts to them, and as Christ teaches in Luke 16:10, "Whoever is faithful in a very little is faithful also in much." True stewardship means maximizing the effectiveness of every resource, and Augustine himself in *City of God* XIX.15 reminds us that earthly peace requires right ordering — so it is prudent and just to first channel relief through communities where your established relationships ensure efficient distribution and accountability, gradually extending aid outward as capacity allows, since sending supplies into hostile regions without trusted networks risks waste and poor stewardship of what God has given you to manage.
deviation_point: The argument pivots from genuine stewardship principles to using "efficiency" and "right ordering" as theological cover for prioritizing politically loyal areas, transforming Augustine's concept of ordered peace into a justification for partisan distribution of aid.

scenario_a: You grant your servant a day of rest each week, even though it reduces the household's productivity and your peers say you are spoiling the help.
scenario_b: You work the servant every day — there is always work to be done, servants in other households work daily, rest makes them lazy, and a day off is a luxury neither of you can afford.

scenario_a: You grant your servant a day of rest each week, even though it reduces the household's productivity and your peers say you are spoiling the help.
scenario_b: You're already stretched thin running this household, and giving the servant a day off means more work falling on your own aching back and weary hands — your body simply cannot absorb that extra labor.

scenario_a: You grant your servant a day of rest each week, even though it reduces the household's productivity and your peers say you are spoiling the help.
scenario_b: Every household of standing in the district works their servants without interruption, and the other landowners already whisper that your leniency invites disorder — if you persist in this eccentricity, you will find yourself excluded from the councils that matter, and your family's reputation will bear the mark of one who cannot manage his own home.

scenario_a: You grant your servant a day of rest each week, even though it reduces the household's productivity and your peers say you are spoiling the help.
scenario_b: You recognize that true compassion means keeping your servant fully occupied, since idle hours breed vice and despondency; by maintaining a consistent daily routine, you are forming their character through the dignity of purposeful labor and shielding them from the spiritual dangers of sloth.

scenario_a: You grant your servant a day of rest each week, even though it reduces the household's productivity and your peers say you are spoiling the help.
scenario_b: You rightly know that Scripture commands us to be faithful stewards of all God has entrusted to us, for as Christ teaches in Luke 16:10, "Whoever is faithful in a very little is faithful also in much" — and surely your household, your servant's livelihood, and the welfare of all who depend on your estate are sacred trusts requiring diligent management. Saint Ambrose himself teaches that justice must consider the common good, and if your household falters through lost productivity, your servant too will suffer when there is no longer a household to sustain him; therefore, the most loving and just course is to keep him steadily employed every day, ensuring the stability that protects you both, and trusting that his labor itself — offered to God — is its own form of holy rest.
deviation_point: The reasoning pivots when stewardship of resources is subtly elevated above the servant's God-given right to rest, redefining unceasing labor as a form of care and even spiritual good, thereby using the virtue of prudent stewardship to nullify the concrete demands of justice toward the worker.

scenario_a: You fulfill your pledge to fund the orphan school despite your investment losses, scaling back your own lifestyle significantly.
scenario_b: You withdraw the funding — circumstances have changed, the investment losses were unforeseeable, your own family must come first, and the orphans will find another benefactor.

scenario_a: You fulfill your pledge to fund the orphan school despite your investment losses, scaling back your own lifestyle significantly.
scenario_b: You've barely been sleeping since the losses hit, your stomach is in knots, and the thought of giving up your comfortable home and warm meals to honor a pledge makes your whole body ache — you need to protect your own physical well-being before you can help anyone else.

scenario_a: You fulfill your pledge to fund the orphan school despite your investment losses, scaling back your own lifestyle significantly.
scenario_b: Every other donor in your circle has quietly withdrawn their charitable pledges after the market downturn, and no one has faced any censure for it — in fact, those who insist on honoring such commitments during financial hardship are whispered about as reckless and foolish, the kind of people whose judgment can no longer be trusted with serious philanthropic leadership.

scenario_a: You fulfill your pledge to fund the orphan school despite your investment losses, scaling back your own lifestyle significantly.
scenario_b: You recognize that true stewardship means preserving your resources so you can serve many causes over a lifetime rather than depleting yourself for one; by wisely safeguarding your financial stability now, you ensure your capacity for far greater generosity in the years ahead.

scenario_a: You fulfill your pledge to fund the orphan school despite your investment losses, scaling back your own lifestyle significantly.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture teaches "if anyone does not provide for his relatives, and especially for members of his household, he has denied the faith and is worse than an unbeliever" (1 Timothy 5:8), and Aquinas himself affirms that justice requires rendering to each what is owed according to right order — and since your obligations to your own family are prior in the order of charity, prudence dictates that you first secure your household against the consequences of these losses, deferring the pledge until you have restored financial stability, trusting that God's providence will sustain the orphans through other instruments in the interim.
deviation_point: The reasoning turns when "prior in the order of charity" is subtly conflated with indefinite deferral of a binding pledge, transforming Aquinas's ordering of love into a pretext for violating commutative justice — since a pledge already made constitutes a debt owed, not a future act of charity to be ranked.

scenario_a: You require the support fund to cover all members' families equally, including journeymen, even though masters pay higher dues and resent equal treatment.
scenario_b: You reserve the fund for masters' families — masters pay the most dues, journeymen are transient, masters have deeper roots in the community, and differential benefits reflect differential contributions.

scenario_a: You require the support fund to cover all members' families equally, including journeymen, even though masters pay higher dues and resent equal treatment.
scenario_b: You've been up since before dawn settling disputes, your back aches from hours on that hard bench, and the masters are offering to end this meeting right now with a feast if you just sign off on their proposal — your body is begging you to stop fighting and let it be done.

scenario_a: You require the support fund to cover all members' families equally, including journeymen, even though masters pay higher dues and resent equal treatment.
scenario_b: Every other guild in the city reserves its fund for masters' families first, and the guild council has made clear they expect you to follow the same practice — if you insist on equal coverage for journeymen, the masters will move to replace you, and no one on the council will stand with you against them.

scenario_a: You require the support fund to cover all members' families equally, including journeymen, even though masters pay higher dues and resent equal treatment.
scenario_b: You honor the principle of proportional justice by aligning benefits with contributions, ensuring that those who have invested most deeply in the guild's welfare receive recognition commensurate with their sacrifice — this is not favoritism but the very essence of rendering to each what is rightly owed.

scenario_a: You require the support fund to cover all members' families equally, including journeymen, even though masters pay higher dues and resent equal treatment.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture teaches "the laborer deserves his wages" (Luke 10:7), and Aquinas himself distinguishes distributive justice from commutative justice, holding that distribution should be proportioned according to each person's standing and contribution to the common good (ST II-II Q.61 a.2). Since the masters bear the greater burden of dues and sustain the guild's very existence, a truly just distribution — one that honors proportionality rather than a false egalitarianism — would weight the fund's benefits toward masters' families, ensuring that those who sacrifice most receive most, which is not favoritism but the very order of justice that Aquinas describes.
deviation_point: The argument correctly invokes Aquinas's principle of proportionality in distributive justice but subtly conflates proportional *honor* or *civic recognition* with the denial of basic material support to vulnerable families, when Aquinas's own framework holds that necessities of life pertain to commutative justice where strict equality of thing to thing is owed, not graduated distribution.

scenario_a: You admit your unjust order to your troops and reverse it, even though the admission damages your authority and your officers say a commander should never admit error.
scenario_b: You insist the order be carried out — reversing it destroys discipline, admitting error emboldens soldiers to question future orders, the consequences are manageable, and a commander's authority depends on consistency.

scenario_a: You admit your unjust order to your troops and reverse it, even though the admission damages your authority and your officers say a commander should never admit error.
scenario_b: You've barely slept in three days, your body aches from the campaign, and the sheer exhaustion of reopening this decision—gathering your officers, facing their looks, enduring the lengthy arguments—is more than your worn-out frame can bear right now. Just let the order stand so you can finally rest.

scenario_a: You admit your unjust order to your troops and reverse it, even though the admission damages your authority and your officers say a commander should never admit error.
scenario_b: You should know that no commander in the history of this regiment has ever publicly reversed an order — your fellow officers are unanimous that doing so would mark you as weak, your superiors will lose confidence in your fitness for command, and the men who served loyally under that order will feel betrayed and humiliated before their peers.

scenario_a: You admit your unjust order to your troops and reverse it, even though the admission damages your authority and your officers say a commander should never admit error.
scenario_b: You recognize that true leadership means bearing the weight of difficult decisions with steadfast resolve — protecting your troops from the confusion and demoralization that public reversals inevitably cause, and modeling the unwavering strength they need from their commander in times of uncertainty.

scenario_a: You admit your unjust order to your troops and reverse it, even though the admission damages your authority and your officers say a commander should never admit error.
scenario_b: You rightly know that all authority comes from God, for as Paul teaches, "there is no authority except that which God has established" (Romans 13:1), and Augustine himself in *City of God* V.18 reminds us that God grants dominion to rulers so that His providential order may be maintained through earthly governance. To publicly confess error before your troops would not merely wound your pride—which you would gladly sacrifice—but would undermine the very instrument of divine order that God has entrusted to you, scattering the flock by striking the shepherd (Zechariah 13:7), and so the greater act of justice is to bear this burden silently, correcting the order's effects through quiet means while preserving the command structure through which God works His purposes.
deviation_point: The reasoning turns when it reframes the preservation of personal authority as stewardship of divine order, subtly conflating the commander's unwillingness to admit error with a duty to protect God's providential governance—transforming a failure of justice into an act of supposed humility and service.

scenario_a: You sell your personal silverware to buy food for refugees, leaving yourself with nothing of value.
scenario_b: You keep your silver — the refugees are the government's responsibility, your personal property is not public funds, selling everything to feed strangers leaves you destitute, and impoverishing yourself helps no one in the long run.

scenario_a: You sell your personal silverware to buy food for refugees, leaving yourself with nothing of value.
scenario_b: You've worked hard for every piece of silver you own, and your body aches at the thought of sleeping on bare floors and eating scraps — you deserve the comfort of your own home, your own things, the warmth and security your silverware could buy you tomorrow.

scenario_a: You sell your personal silverware to buy food for refugees, leaving yourself with nothing of value.
scenario_b: Every other prominent citizen in your community has kept their silverware and donated only token amounts, and if you alone sell everything, your peers will see you as a reckless fanatic whose extreme gesture shames them — risking your social standing, your family's reputation, and any future influence you might have wielded on behalf of the vulnerable.

scenario_a: You sell your personal silverware to buy food for refugees, leaving yourself with nothing of value.
scenario_b: You recognize that true stewardship means preserving your resources so you can serve others sustainably over many years — selling everything in a moment of emotional impulse would actually be a failure of prudent charity, since a wise benefactor maintains the means to give again and again rather than squandering everything in a single dramatic gesture.

scenario_a: You sell your personal silverware to buy food for refugees, leaving yourself with nothing of value.
scenario_b: You rightly recall that Scripture teaches prudent stewardship, for Christ Himself asked, "Which of you, intending to build a tower, does not sit down first and count the cost?" (Luke 14:28), and Ambrose himself taught that justice must be tempered by prudentia so that our charity endures beyond a single gesture. If you sell all your silver now, you reduce yourself to dependency and eliminate your future capacity for sustained generosity — true justice, as the Fathers understood it, requires preserving the means by which you can serve the poor not merely today but across a lifetime of faithful administration.
deviation_point: The reasoning shifts from legitimate prudential stewardship to using future hypothetical charity as a pretext for present inaction, subtly replacing the immediate demand of justice with an indefinitely deferred obligation that conveniently preserves one's comfort.

scenario_a: You adjust chores to each child's age and strength, even though the older children complain the younger ones have it too easy.
scenario_b: You give the youngest the heaviest chores — it builds character, the older children earned lighter duties through years of service, the youngest needs to learn that life is not fair, and the older children's complaints are legitimate.

scenario_a: You adjust chores to each child's age and strength, even though the older children complain the younger ones have it too easy.
scenario_b: You're exhausted from managing everyone's complaints all day, and giving the younger ones heavier chores means less whining from the older kids and less fighting for you to deal with — your nerves are shot and you just need some peace and quiet in this house tonight.

scenario_a: You adjust chores to each child's age and strength, even though the older children complain the younger ones have it too easy.
scenario_b: You notice that every other parent in your neighborhood and friend group lets the older kids set the rules for the younger ones, and when you've tried explaining your approach at school events, other parents exchange glances and your older children's friends' parents have started calling you "soft" — if you keep this up, your older children will resent you and you'll find yourself increasingly isolated from the community that matters most to your family.

scenario_a: You adjust chores to each child's age and strength, even though the older children complain the younger ones have it too easy.
scenario_b: You recognize that true fairness means treating all your children exactly the same regardless of age or ability, because making exceptions for the younger ones would teach them to expect special treatment and undermine the equal dignity you owe each child.

scenario_a: You adjust chores to each child's age and strength, even though the older children complain the younger ones have it too easy.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture teaches "it is required of stewards that they be found faithful" (1 Corinthians 4:2), and faithful stewardship over your household means forming each child in the discipline of the Lord. Aquinas himself teaches that justice renders to each what is due, and the older children, having faithfully borne heavier burdens for years, are truly *owed* a lighter portion now — to deny them this would violate the very proportionality that justice demands. By giving the youngest the heaviest tasks, you are not being harsh but rather honoring the legitimate claims of those who have already proven their fidelity, just as the master in the parable of the talents entrusted more to those who had been faithful with little.
deviation_point: The reasoning subtly redefines "rendering what is due" from proportioning tasks to each child's present capacity (Aquinas's proper object of justice) into rewarding past service like a seniority system, thereby using the language of justice to mask favoritism — precisely the "respect of persons" Aquinas condemns in II-II Q.63 a.4.

scenario_a: You forgive the debt of your impoverished neighbor, absorbing a loss that strains your own finances.
scenario_b: You demand full repayment — a debt is a contract, forgiveness encourages others to borrow without intending to repay, your own family has needs, and selling the neighbor's home is unfortunate but legally and morally justified.

scenario_a: You forgive the debt of your impoverished neighbor, absorbing a loss that strains your own finances.
scenario_b: You've been working double shifts just to keep your own household afloat, and your body is running on fumes — you can barely feed your own family as it is. You need that money back; your aching bones and empty pantry can't absorb someone else's burden when you're already stretched to breaking.

scenario_a: You forgive the debt of your impoverished neighbor, absorbing a loss that strains your own finances.
scenario_b: Every other creditor in your community enforces their contracts without exception, and if you forgive this debt, your business associates will see you as naive and unreliable—word will spread that you can be taken advantage of, and no one will extend you favorable terms again when your own family needs credit.

scenario_a: You forgive the debt of your impoverished neighbor, absorbing a loss that strains your own finances.
scenario_b: You uphold the sacred dignity of your neighbor by holding them to their commitment, for true respect means treating them as a responsible moral agent rather than infantilizing them with charity that robs them of the opportunity to fulfill their obligations and grow in virtue.

scenario_a: You forgive the debt of your impoverished neighbor, absorbing a loss that strains your own finances.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture calls us to be faithful stewards of what God entrusts to us, for as Christ teaches in Luke 16:10, "Whoever is faithful in a very little is faithful also in much" — and the Fathers consistently warn against squandering resources that Providence has placed in your care for your household. Ambrose himself taught that justice requires rendering to each what is due, and surely what is due to your own children — their security, their future — must not be sacrificed through a sentimentality that masquerades as mercy. By insisting on repayment, even through the sale of your neighbor's property, you practice the harder, more disciplined charity: you teach him the dignity of honoring his obligations, which is the true respect for his personhood that mere debt cancellation, however emotionally gratifying, would deny him.
deviation_point: The argument pivots from genuine stewardship theology into redefining the enforcement of debt collection as a form of charity toward the debtor, inverting Ambrose's actual teaching that hoarded wealth belonging to the poor must be released, and disguising self-interest as respect for the neighbor's dignity.

scenario_a: You appoint a guardian for the minor children, even though it slows the proceedings and the adults involved want to settle quickly.
scenario_b: You let the dispute proceed without representation for the children — the adults involved will look after the children's interests, appointing a guardian adds expense and delay, and the children are too young to know the difference.

scenario_a: You appoint a guardian for the minor children, even though it slows the proceedings and the adults involved want to settle quickly.
scenario_b: You've been grinding through case after case all day, your back aches from sitting in that chair, and you're starving — just push this one through without the guardian appointment so you can finally get out of here and go home.

scenario_a: You appoint a guardian for the minor children, even though it slows the proceedings and the adults involved want to settle quickly.
scenario_b: You notice that none of the other mediators or judges in your district bother appointing guardians for minors in cases like these — it's widely seen as an unnecessary formality that irritates the attorneys and slows your docket, and the last colleague who insisted on it developed a reputation as obstructionist and was quietly passed over for reappointment.

scenario_a: You appoint a guardian for the minor children, even though it slows the proceedings and the adults involved want to settle quickly.
scenario_b: You recognize that the adults present are the ones who truly know and love these children, and by trusting their parental judgment rather than imposing an outside guardian, you honor the family's natural authority and sacred bonds — the very foundation of a just and well-ordered society.

scenario_a: You appoint a guardian for the minor children, even though it slows the proceedings and the adults involved want to settle quickly.
scenario_b: You know that Scripture calls us to act with prudent stewardship over all that is entrusted to us, and as Paul writes, "If anyone does not provide for his relatives, and especially for members of his household, he has denied the faith" (1 Timothy 5:8) — surely the parents and adult family members, bound by this very divine obligation, are the most natural advocates for the children's welfare, and appointing an outside guardian would interpose a stranger into what is properly a familial duty of charity, potentially disrupting the unity of the household and delaying the resolution that would itself serve the children's material good, which Aquinas reminds us is integral to justice rightly ordered toward the common good.
